data.title: Texas Roadhouse: Beef Inflation Has Me Raising My Stake
data.description:
Texas Roadhouse stock is stuck

Few companies combine quality customer experience, customer loyalty, operating efficiency, and strategic pricing in the way casual-dining steakhouse chain Texas Roadhouse (NASDAQ:TXRH) does. As a result, I bought it convincingly at the end of 2022, as part of my Christmas present to myself. Back then, the stock was trading at a price of around $90. Since then, it's up 86%, not counting dividends. I was even up over 100% before the stock retraced back a bit. However, most of the gains were made between the Fall of 2023 and the year-end of 2024. Since then, the stock has been stuck between $160 and $200, mostly staying in the $180-200 range. Is this investment dead money? Should we exit our position, enjoying our hefty gains, and move on to better opportunities? After Texas Roadhouse's Q2 report, it seems the market is moving in this direction, as the stock is selling off by almost 6%.

Texas Roadhouse's Q2 Earnings Review

Take a look at the financial highlights Texas Roadhouse reported: sales grew 12.7% YoY to $1.5 billion, driven by a 5.8% comparable sales increase and a 7.2% increase in store weeks. Restaurant margin dollars increased 6.1% thanks to higher sales. In other words, sales were strong. The company beat revenue estimates, although by only $10 million. This means analysts were correctly expecting strong traffic. Now, we don't take this for granted. There are many restaurants that struggle to keep traffic flat YoY. McDonald's, for example, recently reported 3.8% comp sales growth, but it's questionable whether this increase is mainly produced by easy comparables or a true uptick in traffic. Texas Roadhouse simply doesn't have this problem: Customers are flocking to the restaurant, and the to-go business is also thriving. Average unit volume was up 5.1%. In particular, Q2 shows an acceleration since the 26-week result is a cumulative 4.7% in comparable restaurant sales, which means that Q2 was accretive to the total.

However, Texas Roadhouse is exposed to margin pressures. Food inflation is high at 5.2%, which is why restaurant margins decreased 108 bps to 17.1%. Moreover, Texas Roadhouse also had wage inflation of 3.8%. Beef, in particular, is increasing faster than expected because of tight supply, which should reach its peak in Q3 by as much as 7% inflation. But we have signs of herd expansion that could invert the trend starting in Q4 and then through 2026.

Texas Roadhouse is not the kind of company that will immediately raise prices to combat the pressure it feels on its margins. It knows that this is a short-lived fix that would undermine its main asset: the loyalty of its customers. People go to Texas Roadhouse's restaurants because they perceive the value they're getting for the full experience there. As a result, Texas Roadhouse has often accepted a margin hit to keep its customers happy and then applied little price hikes of 1 or 2 percentage points that realign the ratio between sales and its costs. As we can see below, the purple line shows Texas Roadhouse's annual revenue, and the orange line shows the cost of sales. In blue, you find the company's operating margin. You may notice that from 2022 to late 2023, margins decreased a bit while sales were growing. This is what I mean by the company accepting a hit. We were still above a 7.5% operating margin. In the meantime, Texas Roadhouse moved up its prices, and we see that in 2024, margins went up. More importantly, the two lines of its revenues and its COGS don't have the same slope, with the latter growing more slowly than the former. So, when investors worry about Texas Roadhouse's earnings, I think they should look at this chart to understand what is truly going on. In Q3, pricing will be 2.3%, so margins will probably see further pressure. With the roll-offs, Q4 will have 3.1% pricing, which will extend to Q1 2026.

As a side note, Texas Roadhouse told us something important about how consumers are behaving. The company sees a positive entrée mix and bigger steaks ordered. What is pressuring the mix is lower alcohol sales. But this means that customers are not pulling back because of prices (they wouldn't order an entree or a larger steak), but because of a new societal trend (less drinking). When I recently dealt with Constellation Brands (STZ), this was precisely the trend that made me bearish, even before Berkshire's purchase.

The outlook for the remainder of the year is that Q3 comparable sales should keep growing positively since the first five weeks of Q3 reported a 5.3% increase. Commodity inflation is seen at around 5% and wage inflation should be around 4%. While labor is more or less in line with what was expected, food and beverage have increased their weight as a % of restaurant sales and now account for 34%, up 131 bps YoY.

This means we'll probably see slower EPS growth compared to the speed of Texas Roadhouse's top line. In the past two quarters, in particular, EPS growth was mainly driven by a reduced share count since Texas Roadhouse has been purchasing shares at a rate almost twice that of last year ($60.4 million vs. $35.1 million).

As a side note, Texas Roahouse operates two other chains: Bubba's 33 and Jaggers. Bubba's 33 is doing well and has reached 53 locations, mainly in Texas. While it's still dwarfed by the Texas Roadhouse brand, we should start considering the success of this concept. Given the strong track record Texas Roadhouse provides, it should not be a surprise to think that Bubba's 33 could fuel further growth in the next decade and become a true pillar of Texas Roadhouse's narrative and financials.

We should also be aware that Texas Roadhouse often opens franchise locations to grow more rapidly. Then it repurchases this location as part of its capital allocation plan. It's a smart move because it reduces initial capex and helps achieve a faster growth pace. Not only does the company repurchase shares, but it can also reinvest back in itself by gaining direct ownership of its franchised locations.

Valuation

Texas Roadhouse is trading lower mainly because of commodity inflation. Is this a reason to sell? I don't think so, and I ground this on Texas Roadhouse's business culture. The company is not a commodity play, but it's a restaurant offering a satisfying and enjoyable experience that can be repeated. Its core asset is the trustworthy relationship it has established with its customers. This is an asset that defies any margin pressure and secures profitable prospects.

Moreover, the stock's valuation, after having seen a strong multiple expansion during its 2024 run, has been playing catch-up. Its price has been going sideways as the company's earnings grew and compressed the multiple. Right now, we have a PE that is once again below the 3-year average, while the EV to EBITDA ratio is barely above average. Based on historical trends, we have completed the mean reversion that many have waited for.

Analysts are expecting EPS weakness, and this is probably true because of higher inflation. The fwd PE remains a 28. But since many expect 2026 to be more favorable, the company's earnings are forecasted to be $7.55, which immediately brings the PE down to 24.5, below the average you see in the chart. This is a large amount of compression in just one year.

Right now, the valuation still looks steep, but this is linked to the commodity outlook we have discussed above. There's no major issue embedded in the company, which will likely keep growing and attracting new customers. As a result, I see Texas Roadhouse as an opportunity that is served hot off the grill, and I believe that the next three months at least will offer us buying opportunities.
